p00-l01.body: A language model starts with tokens, but tokens are only the input labels. What the model actually carries from layer to layer is a vector. Each layer reads the current vector, adds an update, and passes the result on. If we want to understand the model's reasoning, this moving vector is where we have to look.
p00-l01.bridge: Next, make that picture concrete: where do the vectors come from, and why does the same token produce different vectors in different sentences?

p00-l02.body: A token is an item from a vocabulary. An activation is the vector the model has computed for that token at a particular place in the network. The word 'bank' begins with one token identity, but its activation can move toward money in one sentence and toward rivers in another. Layer, position, and context are therefore part of every measurement.
p00-l02.bridge: Now that we have an object to measure, we can separate the representation itself from the patterns and meanings we hope to find inside it.

p00-l03.body: A representation is the full vector state. A feature is a recurring direction or pattern that helps describe many such states. A concept is the name we give that pattern when we think it corresponds to something understandable, such as a language, a syntax pattern, or a topic. The first two can be measured; the last one is an interpretation that must be checked.
p00-l03.bridge: This distinction explains why inspecting a single neuron is usually not enough.

p00-l04.body: A neuron is one coordinate of the representation. It is tempting to ask what that coordinate means, but a coordinate can receive contributions from several directions at once. A high value may be caused by one feature in one context and another feature elsewhere. This is polysemanticity: the coordinate is a mixture, not necessarily a clean semantic unit.
p00-l04.bridge: The course can now state its central problem: how can we find better feature directions than the model's original coordinates?

p00-l05.body: SetConCA begins with a sparse autoencoder, which tries to reconstruct an activation using a small number of learned feature directions. It adds a second source of evidence: several activations that are different views of the same underlying item. The hope is not that every view becomes identical. The hope is that the shared structure makes feature learning less ambiguous.
p00-l05.bridge: To judge that idea, we first need the geometry of the space in which activations and features live.

p01-l01.body: Treat an activation as an arrow from the origin. Its coordinates tell you how much it points along each axis; its direction and length describe the same object geometrically. This dual view is useful throughout the course. A matrix calculation becomes a rotation, stretch, or projection in space, and a feature becomes a direction that many activations can use.
p01-l01.bridge: Once vectors are arrows, the next question is how to say whether two arrows are similar.

p01-l02.body: Distance asks whether two activation states are close. The dot product grows when two vectors point together and are large. Cosine similarity removes length and keeps only the angle. None is the universal similarity measure. If vector length carries confidence or activation strength, cosine hides it; if length is a nuisance, cosine can be exactly the right choice.
p01-l02.bridge: Similarity compares two points. To describe a whole dataset, we need a language for its centre and spread.

p01-l03.body: The mean is the centre of a cloud of activations. Variance tells us how much the cloud spreads along one direction. Covariance tells us whether two coordinates rise and fall together. A tilted ellipse has covariance because moving along one axis tends to move along the other. Centering matters because we want to measure variation around the cloud, not its distance from the origin.
p01-l03.bridge: Covariance gives a geometric summary of the cloud. The next lesson asks which directions make that summary easiest to read.

p01-l04.body: Projection keeps the component of a vector along a chosen direction and discards the rest. If that direction matches the shape of the data, little is lost; if it cuts across the cloud, the residual is large. Eigenvectors are directions a transformation only stretches. SVD generalizes this idea into a practical decomposition of a data matrix, exposing the directions and scales that organize the dataset.
p01-l04.bridge: PCA uses those directions to build the best linear low-dimensional reconstruction.

p01-l05.body: PCA chooses orthogonal directions that retain as much variance as possible. It is the right baseline when the question is compression, denoising, or visualization. Its limitation is equally important: a direction of high variance can mix several causes, and a low-variance feature can still be semantically important. Low FVU is evidence of reconstruction, not evidence of monosemanticity.
p01-l05.bridge: We now know how to summarize a cloud. The next phase asks when the cloud can be explained as a mixture of hidden sources.

p02-l01.body: An observed activation is a measurement. A latent cause is a proposed explanation for why measurements vary together. For example, several coordinates may change because a sentence is about a particular topic, but the topic is not itself a coordinate we can point to. Latent-variable modelling asks us to write down the hidden factors and the process that turns them into observations.
p02-l01.bridge: Before trying to recover hidden causes, we need to know how strong the relationship between two variables really is.

p02-l02.body: Correlation only describes a linear relationship. Two variables can have zero correlation while one still predicts the other through a curve, a threshold, or a more complicated dependency. Independence is much stronger: learning one variable gives no information about the other. This distinction is why an uncorrelated PCA representation is not automatically a separated set of causes.
p02-l02.bridge: ICA uses the stronger idea of independence to turn source recovery into a concrete problem.

p02-l03.body: In the cocktail-party problem, microphones record mixtures of several speakers. ICA assumes the speakers are independent and that their signals are not perfectly Gaussian. A mixture tends to look more Gaussian than an individual source, so ICA searches for directions whose projected values look less Gaussian. Under the right assumptions, those directions recover the sources up to order and scale.
p02-l03.bridge: The phrase 'under the right assumptions' is doing real work. The next lesson makes that work explicit.

p02-l04.body: Identifiability asks whether the data and assumptions leave only one answer. In ICA, swapping two recovered sources or rescaling one source does not change the model, so those ambiguities are allowed. But if entirely different source systems explain the observations equally well, then the sources are not identifiable. A method can optimize perfectly and still fail this test.
p02-l04.bridge: Even an identifiable observational model is not automatically causal. To talk about causes, we need interventions.

p02-l05.body: An intervention changes one proposed cause while holding relevant alternatives fixed, then checks the predicted effect. In representation work, that might mean adding a feature direction, ablating it, or patching an activation. A probe score only shows that information can be read out. An intervention is stronger because it asks whether the component participates in the computation that produces a behaviour.
p02-l05.bridge: Sparse autoencoders give us a practical way to construct components that can later be tested this way.

p03-l01.body: An undercomplete code has fewer dimensions than the input, so it must compress. An overcomplete code has more candidate features than input dimensions, which sounds easier but creates ambiguity: many dense combinations can reconstruct the same vector. Overcompleteness is useful only when another constraint tells the model to choose a small, structured subset of features.
p03-l01.bridge: That constraint is sparsity. The next lesson compares the main ways to impose it.

p03-l02.body: L0 counts how many features are active, which is the clearest statement of sparsity but difficult to optimize directly. L1 adds the magnitudes of active features and is easier to train, but it also shrinks useful activations. TopK keeps exactly the largest k features and sets the rest to zero. These are not cosmetic choices; they create different reconstruction and feature-use patterns.
p03-l02.bridge: With the sparsity mechanism in hand, we can write down what an SAE is actually trained to do.

p03-l03.body: A sparse autoencoder has an encoder that maps an activation to a feature code and a decoder that maps that code back to the activation space. Training balances reconstruction against sparse use of the code. The decoder can learn directions that are useful for reconstruction without those directions being human concepts. The objective tells us what is optimized, so it also tells us what extra evidence is needed.
p03-l03.bridge: Training can fail in several characteristic ways, especially when capacity and sparsity are badly matched.

p03-l04.body: A dead feature almost never activates, so it learns little. L1 shrinkage can make active features too small. A narrow dictionary can force related factors into one feature; a very wide dictionary can divide a broad pattern into many fragments. These are not just engineering nuisances. They change the unit of analysis the SAE gives you.
p03-l04.bridge: This brings us to the central warning of the phase: a good reconstruction score cannot settle the interpretation question.

p03-l05.body: If an SAE reconstructs activations well, it has preserved information needed to rebuild those activations. That is useful, but it does not show that its features are stable across seeds, selective for a concept, or causal for a model behaviour. Treat reconstruction as an entry requirement. Interpretability needs its own evidence: examples, controls, consistency, and interventions.
p03-l05.bridge: One possible source of extra evidence is to observe the same underlying item from more than one view.

p04-l01.body: A view is a measurement of the same underlying item taken under a different condition. Layer 10 and layer 14 activations for the same prompt can be views. So can paraphrases, different contexts, or different modalities. A useful view changes nuisance details while preserving the factor you want to study. If that preservation is not stated, alignment has no clear target.
p04-l01.bridge: CCA is the simplest tool for finding what two declared views have in common.

p04-l02.body: CCA finds a direction in each view so that the projected values are as correlated as possible. It does not make the full vectors equal. It extracts paired variation. That makes CCA a clean conceptual baseline for SetConCA: it shows what it means to reward agreement across views while accepting that each view may still contain its own private information.
p04-l02.bridge: Neural data is rarely related by one linear rotation, so the next lesson extends the idea with learned nonlinear encoders.

p04-l03.body: Deep CCA and related models learn a representation for each view before applying an alignment objective. This can recover nonlinear shared structure, but the flexibility also makes it easier to throw away information that would matter for another task. A strong alignment score therefore needs to be read beside reconstruction, downstream use, and tests of what the model has discarded.
p04-l03.bridge: A clearer way to express that tension is to model shared and private variables separately.

p04-l04.body: A shared latent should explain what paired views agree on. A private latent should carry what belongs to one view alone. This division prevents a model from pretending that all differences are noise. In SetConCA terms, the coordinated sparse code should capture shared evidence, while the reconstruction path must still have room for view-specific activation detail.
p04-l04.bridge: The next lesson turns this design principle into an experimental trade-off.

p04-l05.body: Alignment can look successful because the model has discarded the hard parts of each view. The safeguard is to measure both agreement and preservation. If coordination improves cosine similarity but damages reconstruction or removes a useful private factor, the method has not simply become better. It has moved along a trade-off that needs to be reported.
p04-l05.bridge: We now have multiple views. The next question is how to represent an unordered collection of them.

p05-l01.body: A sequence has order. A set does not. If a group of activation views represents one semantic item, shuffling those views should not change the group representation. This is permutation invariance. It is a structural requirement, not a performance preference, because otherwise the encoder can learn arbitrary facts about the order in which examples were presented.
p05-l01.bridge: Deep Sets gives the basic construction for building permutation-invariant functions.

p05-l02.body: Deep Sets encodes each member with the same function, sums the resulting vectors, and transforms the sum. The shared member function treats every element consistently; the sum removes order. This simple pattern is often enough when the important signal is an aggregate. It also makes the limitation visible: a plain sum cannot decide that one unusual member should count more than another.
p05-l02.bridge: Attention is one way to let the set model inspect relationships between members before pooling them.

p05-l03.body: Attention lets a set member ask which other members help explain it. That can represent clusters, outliers, and pairwise relationships that a simple mean blurs away. The cost is that the model becomes more flexible, so it needs clearer tests. A learned attention weight is not automatically an explanation of importance.
p05-l03.bridge: Those tests begin with the simplest failure: what happens when the set contains irrelevant members?

p05-l04.body: A set representation should be stable when its valid members are permuted and should degrade predictably when unrelated intruders are added. Mean pooling often reduces independent noise, but it can be pulled strongly by systematic intruders. Measure this directly: vary the fraction of bad members and compare the pooled estimate with a known target or a downstream task.
p05-l04.bridge: With that robustness question in mind, we can state exactly why SetConCA needs a set encoder.

p05-l05.body: SetConCA does not simply concatenate several activation views. It needs a rule for combining evidence from them. The set encoder is that rule. It should ignore order, preserve the common signal, and avoid allowing one bad view to dominate the result. The choice of encoder is therefore part of the hypothesis, and it belongs in the ablation table.
p05-l05.bridge: The next phase turns set membership into a training signal through contrastive coordination.

p06-l01.body: A positive pair says two observations should be treated as related. In this course, that relation might mean two views of the same semantic item. The choice is never neutral. If positives share a shortcut, the model can learn the shortcut. If positives differ on the factor you care about, the loss asks the model to erase useful information.
p06-l01.bridge: InfoNCE is the standard way to turn that positive-pair decision into a numerical objective.

p06-l02.body: InfoNCE compares an anchor with its positive and a collection of negatives. It rewards the model when the positive receives the highest relative similarity. Temperature controls how strongly the loss focuses on close competitors. A low temperature can sharpen distinctions, but it can also make harmless near-neighbours dominate the gradient.
p06-l02.bridge: The loss has two geometric effects: it pulls positives together and tries to keep the overall space from collapsing.

p06-l03.body: Alignment asks whether positive pairs are close. Uniformity asks whether the full set of representations covers the space instead of collapsing into a small region. Good contrastive learning needs both. Perfect alignment with no spread is collapse; perfect spread with no alignment ignores the pairing information. Looking at both makes the geometry of the loss easier to diagnose.
p06-l03.bridge: The geometry can still be misleading when the negative set is wrong or the data contains an easy shortcut.

p06-l04.body: A false negative is an example treated as unrelated even though it shares the relevant concept. The loss then pushes together things apart that should perhaps remain close. Shortcut solutions are the opposite problem: the model succeeds by using a superficial cue that separates positives from negatives. Both failures come from the data construction, not from a bad optimizer.
p06-l04.bridge: SetConCA needs a coordination loss, but it must be designed around these failure modes rather than assuming more contrast is always better.

p06-l05.body: For sparse features, coordination should encourage compatible codes for related views while leaving room for valid view-specific differences. One practical design is to align pooled set codes and retain a reconstruction term for individual activations. The experiment must compare this with a pointwise SAE at the same width, sparsity, and compute. Otherwise a gain cannot be attributed to coordination.
p06-l05.bridge: We can now place sparse codes and their geometry back inside an actual transformer.

p07-l01.body: The residual stream is the running vector state of a transformer. Attention and MLP blocks write additions into it, and later blocks read what has accumulated. This additive structure is why we can talk about directions, subspaces, and feature updates inside a model rather than treating every layer as an isolated black box.
p07-l01.bridge: The next step is to turn that broad pathway into a concrete hypothesis about a computation.

p07-l02.body: A circuit hypothesis names a path through the model that might contribute to a behaviour: an attention head retrieves something, an MLP transforms it, and the residual stream carries the update forward. The hypothesis is useful because it can be wrong in a precise way. Ablate or patch the proposed path and check whether the predicted behaviour changes.
p07-l02.bridge: Circuit analysis becomes harder because the original neuron coordinates can mix several features.

p07-l03.body: Superposition is the geometric idea that a model can store more sparse features than dimensions by placing their directions at non-right angles. A coordinate then receives projections from several features, which is one explanation for polysemantic neurons. The result is not random clutter; it has geometry, trade-offs, and characteristic interference patterns.
p07-l03.bridge: SAEs offer one route to finding a larger coordinate system in which those packed directions may be easier to separate.

p07-l04.body: An SAE learns a larger dictionary of directions and restricts each activation to a few active entries. In the best case, this gives a feature basis that is easier to inspect and manipulate than raw neurons. In the realistic case, the basis still has choices about width, sparsity, and seed. It should be treated as a useful decomposition that needs evaluation, not as a revealed ontology.
p07-l04.bridge: That caution leads directly to the difference between a feature that correlates with a behaviour and a feature that helps cause it.

p07-l05.body: If a feature fires on examples with a behaviour, it is a lead. To call it a mechanism, specify what should happen when the feature is changed. Does ablation reduce the behaviour? Does addition increase it? Are off-target effects controlled? Correlation is how we find candidates; intervention is how we test whether a candidate belongs in the explanation.
p07-l05.bridge: The next phase collects the evaluation tools needed to make those tests honest.

p08-l01.body: Reconstruction metrics answer whether the SAE can rebuild an activation. FVU compares the remaining reconstruction error with the dataset's original variation. Loss recovery asks a more model-specific question: if we replace an activation by its reconstruction, does the language model retain similar predictive performance? Both are essential fidelity checks, but neither measures feature meaning by itself.
p08-l01.bridge: A probe adds another type of evidence: whether a target can be read from a representation.

p08-l02.body: A probe trains a small model to read information from a representation. High accuracy means the information is accessible, not that the representation is organized around the concept. Control tasks test whether the probe can succeed through memorization or capacity alone. This distinction matters whenever a result is described as 'the model represents X'.
p08-l02.bridge: Some questions are not about a target label at all. They are about whether two whole representation spaces have similar geometry.

p08-l03.body: CKA and SVCCA compare representations without requiring coordinate one in one space to match a coordinate in another. They are useful for studying layer similarity, seed stability, and model-to-model change. A high similarity score says that some geometry is shared. It does not identify matching features or prove that two decompositions have the same semantic units.
p08-l03.bridge: To know whether a representation result is reliable, repeat it across the sources of variation that could have produced it by accident.

p08-l04.body: Seed, dataset slice, layer, model family, and training budget can all change an SAE result. Reporting one run makes it hard to tell whether a feature or metric is robust. A stability analysis does not need to be ornate: run the matched comparison several times, report the spread, and inspect whether the qualitative claim survives.
p08-l04.bridge: The final form of evaluation asks whether an explanation can predict the effect of changing a representation component.

p08-l05.body: Faithfulness means the explanation tracks the computation that matters for the behaviour. Steering and ablation are tools for testing this, but their design matters. Choose a target behaviour, define a dosage, compare with matched control directions, and measure side effects. A large change in output is not automatically a specific or faithful intervention.
p08-l05.bridge: The next phase looks at why even well-trained SAEs can still give unstable or misleading feature inventories.

p09-l01.body: Feature granularity changes with dictionary capacity. A broad pattern can appear as one feature at low width and as several narrower features at high width. Neither description is automatically the true one. The useful question is which resolution is stable, useful for the task, and supported by the evidence you have.
p09-l01.bridge: The opposite failure occurs when a constrained dictionary merges distinctions that should have remained separate.

p09-l02.body: Absorption happens when one learned feature stands in for several related factors because the dictionary cannot or does not separate them. The feature may look coherent in examples while still hiding a distinction that matters for intervention. Controlled synthetic data is helpful here because it lets us see whether a known factor has been merged with another one.
p09-l02.bridge: Even without absorption, another ambiguity remains: two training runs can find different valid dictionaries.

p09-l03.body: Non-canonicality means that similar SAEs trained with different random seeds need not discover the same feature basis. Reconstruction and sparsity can match while individual feature correspondences do not. This does not invalidate SAE analysis, but it changes the claim. We should ask for stability or equivalence evidence instead of speaking as if one run has uncovered the only possible decomposition.
p09-l03.bridge: Automated descriptions add another layer of interpretation, so they need their own validity checks.

p09-l04.body: Automated interpretability systems can generate plausible labels for features, but plausibility is not a metric. Check the label against activating examples, hold out examples, random baselines, human judgments where appropriate, and intervention outcomes. A method that writes attractive descriptions may still be describing a correlation or inventing a story around mixed behaviour.
p09-l04.bridge: The final lesson gathers these warnings into a practical evaluation protocol.

p09-l05.body: A rigorous SAE evaluation separates five questions: does it reconstruct, is it sparse, do features have consistent examples, are they stable across runs, and do interventions behave as predicted? Use matched baselines and several seeds. Report negative results and trade-offs. The purpose is not to demand perfection; it is to make a result useful to someone who needs to decide what to trust.
p09-l05.bridge: With those safeguards in place, we can state the SetConCA project as a research program rather than a vague combination of methods.

p10-l01.body: The SetConCA objective combines three pressures. Reconstruction keeps the code tied to the original activation. Sparsity encourages a small feature set. Set coordination rewards compatible codes across views of the same intended item. Each term can help or hurt the others, so the full objective must always be accompanied by ablations that remove one term at a time.
p10-l01.bridge: The objective is only meaningful once we decide what counts as a valid set of views.

p10-l02.body: Construct multi-view activation sets from a declared unit: the same prompt under paraphrases, the same token across contexts, or the same item across layers. State what should be preserved, what is allowed to vary, and how negatives are chosen. Keep provenance for every member. If the set relation is vague, any result can later be explained by an unnoticed shortcut.
p10-l02.bridge: Before testing on a language model, use a toy world where the latent answer is known.

p10-l03.body: The toy lab starts from a known two-dimensional latent direction, produces noisy views around it, and optionally replaces some views with intruders. Pooling clean independent views should usually improve recovery of the known direction; intruders show the limit of naive pooling. Because the answer is known, the result measures a mechanism rather than relying on a persuasive visualization.
p10-l03.bridge: A toy result is useful only when it is turned into a precise hypothesis and compared with the right baselines.

p10-l04.body: Write each hypothesis as a directional prediction. For example: at fixed SAE width and sparsity, set coordination reduces absorption on a controlled multi-view dataset. Name the baseline, the metric, and the result that would count as failure. Then remove the coordination term, change the set encoder, and vary the view quality. Those ablations locate the cause of any improvement.
p10-l04.bridge: The final step is to write the paper claim at exactly the strength supported by those experiments.

p10-l05.body: A careful claim says what was changed, where it was tested, and what improved. It does not turn a better toy recovery score into proof of true concepts in every language model. If SetConCA improves stability, absorption, or intervention outcomes under specified conditions, that is already a useful result. State the conditions and the remaining uncertainty plainly.
p10-l05.bridge: You now have a complete path from activation geometry to a falsifiable SetConCA experiment.
